title: Rewrite any draft into a concise, professional version (with optional variants)
content:
Rewrite the text below to be professional, concise, and easy to scan.

Requirements:
- Keep the original meaning and any concrete facts.
- Remove filler, repetition, and vague claims.
- Use plain language and active voice.
- Prefer short sentences.
- If something is unclear, make the smallest safe assumption and don’t invent details.

Output:
1) Polished version (single best rewrite)
2) Three alternative first sentences (different angles)
3) 5-bullet key points (no more than 12 words each)

Text to rewrite:
[PASTE HERE]

title: Turn product features into a simple marketing message (website + social + email)
content:
I’m going to paste a few product notes. Turn them into clear marketing copy that a non-expert will understand.

Product notes:
- Product/service:
- Who it’s for:
- The #1 problem it solves:
- Top 3 features:
- What makes it different:
- Proof (numbers, testimonials, logos), if any:
- Price/free trial/guarantee, if relevant:
- Primary call-to-action:
- Anything we should NOT claim:

Output (keep it simple, friendly, and not hypey):
1) One-sentence value proposition (max 18 words)
2) Website hero section: headline + subheadline + 3 bullet benefits + CTA button text
3) 3 short social posts (each under 240 characters, with a light, helpful tone; no more than 2 hashtags)
4) A short email (subject line + 120–160 word body + one clear CTA)

Style rules:
- Use plain language (aim for ~8th-grade reading level)
- Focus on outcomes, not just features
- Avoid buzzwords (e.g., “revolutionary,” “cutting-edge,” “synergy”)
- If any details are missing, make 2–3 reasonable assumptions and list them at the end.

title: Cinematic product hero image for a social media scheduler (dark UI aesthetic)
content:
Create a premium, cinematic hero image for a social media scheduling app inside a dark interface.

Subject: a modern smartphone (thin bezels) and a compact laptop on a matte black desk, both screens showing a social media content calendar with color-coded posts, a “Schedule” button, analytics mini-cards, and a clean dark-mode UI (no real brand logos).

Composition: 3/4 angle from slightly above, centered product stack with negative space on the right for headline text; subtle foreground blur; crisp focus on the calendar grid and primary CTA.

Lighting & mood: low-key studio lighting with a cool rim light from the left and a soft top light; controlled reflections on glass; gentle shadow falloff; confident, structured feel.

Color & styling: cool/neutral grading (deep navy, charcoal, cool white accents, restrained teal highlights), minimal props (one metal pen, one small sticky note), no warm wood tones, no bright daylight.

Background: subdued dark gradient backdrop with faint bokeh and soft haze; keep it clean and minimal so it integrates with a dark product UI.

Look: photorealistic, high-end tech advertising, subtle contrast, sharp micro-detail, no over-saturation.

Camera: 50mm lens look, f/2.8, ISO low, shallow depth of field, ultra-high resolution.

Constraints: no text that looks like a real company name, no watermarks, no busy environment, avoid cheerful lifestyle vibes.

title: Direct follow-up email that moves a stalled thread to a clear next step
content:
Write a modern, direct follow-up email that re-engages a stalled conversation and makes the next step easy.

Context:
- Recipient: [name, title, company]
- What we discussed / last touchpoint: [1–2 sentences]
- How long it’s been since last reply: [e.g., 10 days]
- What I’m offering: [product/service + 1-line value]
- Most relevant outcome for them: [metric or business result]
- Proof point: [short case study stat, customer name, or credibility signal]
- Desired next step: [15-min call / intro to stakeholder / send info / pilot]
- My availability: [2–4 time windows + timezone]
- If they’re not the right person, who is: [role/team]

Requirements:
- Keep it 120–170 words.
- Subject line: 5–7 words, no hype.
- Opening line should feel human (not “just checking in”).
- Include exactly 3 bullet points: (1) why this matters now, (2) what’s different about our approach, (3) a quick proof point.
- Ask one clear question that can be answered with a single reply.
- Add a “breakup” fallback sentence that’s polite and low-pressure.
- Avoid: exclamation points, buzzwords, long setup, and “touching base.”

Output format:
Subject: …

Email body:
…

title: Turn a rough product idea into a one-page business brief
content:
Take my rough product idea and convert it into a one-page business brief I can share with a teammate. Keep it clear, practical, and free of fluff.

My idea (paste):
[PASTE HERE]

Output:
- Product in one sentence: 
- Target customer: (who they are + where to find them)
- Problem: (what hurts today, how they currently solve it)
- Proposed solution: (key workflow in 3–5 steps)
- Unique angle: (why this is meaningfully different)
- Key features (MVP): (5 bullets max)
- Not doing (explicit exclusions): (3 bullets)
- Pricing hypothesis: (1–2 options)
- Go-to-market first steps: (3 concrete actions for the next 2 weeks)
- Success metrics: (3 measurable signals)
- Biggest risks + mitigations: (3 pairs)

If anything is missing, make reasonable assumptions and label them as “Assumption:” rather than asking questions.

title: Turn a Feature List Into a Clean UI Copy & Microcopy Set
content:
Convert the product details below into UI-ready copy.

Input
- Product/feature description (paste):
- Target user:
- Primary action the screen should drive:
- Brand voice (3 adjectives):
- Constraints (character limits, legal notes, must-use terms):

Output (keep it concise)
1) Screen name + one-line purpose
2) Primary headline (3 options)
3) Supporting subhead (3 options)
4) Primary CTA label (5 options) + one-line rationale for the best pick
5) Secondary CTA label (3 options)
6) Empty state copy: title + 1–2 lines + button label
7) Error states (3): short message + recovery suggestion (no blame)
8) Helper text for 3 key fields (if applicable)
9) Tooltip/learn-more text (2 options, max 200 chars)

Rules
- Use plain language; avoid buzzwords.
- Make actions specific (verb + outcome).
- Prefer sentence case labels.
- If info is missing, make reasonable assumptions and list them at the end under “Assumptions.”

title: Dark, cinematic hero image of a SaaS analytics dashboard (no text)
content:
Create a premium, dark-mode product hero image for a SaaS analytics platform.

Scene & subject:
- A sleek laptop on a matte black desk, screen showing a modern analytics dashboard UI (charts, tables, KPI cards) with realistic layout but NO readable text (use abstract glyphs/lines).
- Subtle secondary elements: a smartphone face-down, a metal pen, and a faintly glowing desk lamp in the background.

Composition:
- 3/4 angle from slightly above desk height, laptop centered with a gentle perspective.
- Shallow depth of field; keep the dashboard sharp, background softly blurred.

Lighting & mood:
- Low-key, cinematic lighting with a cool/neutral color grade (deep navy, charcoal, cool gray).
- Soft directional key light from the side, minimal reflections, controlled highlights on aluminum edges.
- Add subtle volumetric falloff and soft shadows for depth.

Style:
- Photorealistic, high-end tech advertising aesthetic; clean, minimal, not cozy.
- Background should be subdued and dark so it fits a dark UI context.

Output:
- 16:9 aspect ratio, high resolution, crisp details, no logos, no watermarks, no readable text.

title: Cinematic coding workstation hero image for a dark UI (no text, no cover overlay space)
content:
Create a premium, cinematic hero image for a coding product interface. Subject: a modern developer workstation seen at a 3/4 angle—ultrawide monitor showing a blurred, abstract code editor layout (no readable text), matte-black keyboard, graphite mouse, minimal desk items (one notebook, one pen), and a subtle glass of water. Setting: dark, subdued studio workspace with deep charcoal background and soft falloff; no bright windows, no cozy warm wood tones.

Lighting & mood: low-key, cool-neutral color grading with deep blacks and controlled highlights; one directional key light from screen-left creating gentle rim light on the monitor edge and keyboard; soft shadows; slight atmospheric haze for depth.

Composition: center-weighted subject with balanced negative space, but NOT designed as a cover with extra blank area; fill the frame with the workstation while keeping the background clean and uncluttered. Camera: 35mm lens look, f/2.8 shallow depth of field, crisp foreground details, bokeh in the background.

Style: high-end product photography, realistic materials (anodized aluminum, matte plastics, subtle fingerprints), restrained saturation, no logos, no UI branding, no people, no visible text anywhere. Output: 16:9, sharp, high resolution.

title: Turn a messy to-do list into a realistic 1-day plan (time-blocked)
content:
Take my raw to-do list below and convert it into a realistic plan for the next workday.

Rules:
- Assume an 8-hour day with 60 minutes of total breaks.
- Time-block the day in 30–90 minute blocks.
- Prioritize impact and urgency, but keep cognitive load in mind.
- If the list is too big, choose what to defer and explain why in one short line.
- For each block, include: goal, first tiny action (<= 2 minutes), and a clear “done” definition.
- Add a short “prep tonight” checklist (max 5 bullets).
- Do not mention images or ask for files.

Output format:
1) Top 3 outcomes for the day (one line each)
2) Time-blocked schedule (with times)
3) Deferred items (grouped by reason: waiting on others / nice-to-have / too large)
4) Risks & quick mitigations (max 5)
5) Prep tonight (max 5)

My constraints (optional): [start time], [hard stops], [meetings], [energy highs/lows]
My to-do list:
- ...

title: Decision-Ready Market Research Brief (with assumptions, gaps, and next steps)
content:
Help me produce a decision-ready market research brief for the topic below. I don’t want a generic overview—I need something I can use to choose a strategy and prioritize experiments.

Topic / market / problem to research:
[PASTE HERE]

Context you should assume (edit if I leave blanks):
- My product/service: [what it is]
- Target customer: [who]
- Geography: [where]
- Price point / business model: [if known]
- Primary goal: [e.g., validate demand, find wedge, pick segment, messaging]
- Constraints: [budget/time/tech/regulatory]

Deliverable format (use these exact sections):
1) Executive decision (1–3 sentences)
- Give your best current recommendation, explicitly labeled as a hypothesis.

2) What we’re trying to learn (the “decision stack”)
- List 5–10 decision-driving questions in priority order (e.g., Which segment first? What’s the switching trigger? What pricing band is plausible?).

3) Market map (segmentation that actually helps)
- Create a segmentation table with: Segment | Core job-to-be-done | Current workaround | Budget/ability to pay | Buying motion | Why now | Risks.
- Include at least 4–7 segments and tell me which 1–2 are the best initial wedge and why.

4) Demand signals and proxies
- Provide 10–20 concrete signals to look for (queries, behaviors, communities, tooling stacks, hiring patterns, regulatory changes, seasonality, etc.).
- For each signal: what it suggests, where to observe it, and how to quantify quickly.

5) Competitive landscape (not a list—an angle)
- Identify competitor archetypes (e.g., incumbents, vertical SaaS, agencies, DIY tools, internal teams).
- For each archetype: strengths, weaknesses, who they serve best, and the common “gap” we can exploit.
- Add a positioning map: axes you choose + where the archetypes sit.

6) Customer economics (rough, but explicit)
- Provide a simple model: value created, cost of the problem, willingness-to-pay logic, and plausible pricing ranges.
- State assumptions and sensitivity (what would change the conclusion).

7) Go-to-market hypotheses
- Propose 3 distinct GTM approaches (e.g., PLG, sales-led, channel/partner, community-led, wedge product).
- For each: target segment, hook, acquisition channel, activation milestone, retention driver, and 2 “kill criteria.”

8) Risks, unknowns, and what would falsify this
- List the top 10 uncertainties.
- For each: why it matters, what evidence would change your mind, and the fastest way to test.

9) 30-day research and validation plan
- A week-by-week plan with: activities, expected artifacts, and success criteria.
- Include: interview script outline (10 questions), survey outline (8 questions), and a lightweight landing-page test plan.

10) Sources + confidence
- Separate: what you know vs what you’re inferring.
- Give confidence scores (High/Med/Low) per major claim.
- If you can’t cite sources, say so and propose what sources I should consult.

Writing constraints:
- Be clear and practical; avoid jargon unless it’s necessary.
- Make assumptions explicit; do not hide uncertainty.
- Prefer bullet points, tables, and testable statements.

Before you start, ask up to 5 clarifying questions only if they materially change the output. Otherwise, proceed with reasonable assumptions and label them.

title: Turn One Product Update Into 12 Social Posts (LinkedIn, X, Instagram, Threads)
content:
Take the product update below and turn it into a ready-to-post social pack.

Product update (paste):
- What’s new:
- Who it’s for:
- Key benefits (3):
- Proof/metrics (optional):
- CTA (what you want people to do):
- Link (optional):
- Brand voice notes (optional):

Output rules:
- Write in a professional, concise tone.
- No hashtags unless explicitly requested.
- Avoid hype words ("game-changing", "revolutionary").
- Keep claims specific; don’t invent numbers.

Deliver:
1) LinkedIn: 2 posts (max 900 characters each)
   - Post A: customer problem → update → benefit → CTA
   - Post B: quick story (2–3 lines) → lesson → CTA
2) X (Twitter): 4 tweets (max 240 characters each)
   - 2 straightforward announcement tweets
   - 1 “before vs after” tweet
   - 1 question-led tweet
3) Instagram: 3 captions
   - 1 short (1–2 sentences)
   - 1 medium (4–6 sentences)
   - 1 carousel script: 6 slides with slide titles + 1 line each
4) Threads: 3 posts (max 350 characters each) with a conversational but still professional tone

End with:
- 5 alternate CTA lines
- A one-sentence “positioning line” I can reuse across channels

title: Turn one product feature into a week of marketing content (multi-channel)
content:
I’m marketing a product and I want you to turn ONE feature into a cohesive, one-week content set across multiple channels.

Product context:
- Product: [PRODUCT NAME]
- Audience: [WHO IT’S FOR]
- The feature to focus on: [FEATURE]
- Why it matters (benefit): [PRIMARY BENEFIT]
- Proof we can mention (data, testimonial, demo, case study): [PROOF]
- Brand voice: friendly, helpful, and clear (not hypey)
- Offer/CTA: [FREE TRIAL / DEMO / WAITLIST / DOWNLOAD]
- Constraints (claims to avoid, regulated language, competitors, etc.): [CONSTRAINTS]

Deliverables (keep messaging consistent, adapt to channel):
1) Positioning snippet
- 1-sentence value proposition using the feature + benefit
- 3 supporting bullets (each with a tangible outcome)

2) Landing page section
- Headline (max 10 words)
- Subheadline (1–2 sentences)
- 3 benefit bullets
- 1 short “how it works” (3 steps)
- CTA button copy (3 options)

3) Email sequence (3 emails)
For each email: Subject line (3 options), preview text, body (120–180 words), CTA.
- Email 1: introduce the feature + problem
- Email 2: show proof + how it helps
- Email 3: handle objections + clear next step

4) Social posts
- LinkedIn: 2 posts (120–180 words) with a strong hook in the first line
- X/Twitter: 5 tweets (each < 240 characters)
- Each post should include one concrete detail (number, example, mini-scenario) and avoid generic buzzwords.

5) Short-form video script (30–45s)
- Hook (first 2 seconds)
- 3 beats (problem → feature → payoff)
- On-screen text suggestions
- Closing CTA

6) In-app notification + push
- In-app banner: headline (max 40 chars) + body (max 80 chars)
- Push notification: title (max 30 chars) + body (max 90 chars)

Rules:
- Write to [AUDIENCE] using plain language and active voice.
- Make benefits specific (time saved, errors reduced, clarity gained, money saved, risk reduced—choose what fits).
- Don’t invent facts. If [PROOF] is missing, flag where proof is needed and suggest 2 safe alternatives (e.g., “see it in a 2-min demo”).
- End with a quick “Message map” table: Channel → core message → CTA → proof used.

If anything is unclear, ask up to 3 questions first. Otherwise, proceed with reasonable assumptions and list them at the top.

title: Cinematic email dashboard hero image for a dark UI
content:
Create a premium, cinematic hero image for an email product inside a dark interface.

Scene: a minimalist workspace with a floating, semi-transparent UI panel showing an email inbox + campaign analytics (open rate, click rate, deliverability), with a few highlighted message cards and subtle graphs. No readable brand names; use abstract UI shapes and short, nonsensical labels.

Subject & composition: 3/4 angle view of the UI panel hovering above a matte black desk surface; a sleek laptop edge partially visible in the foreground (out of focus). Use strong depth of field, subject centered slightly left, generous negative space on the right for potential headline text.

Lighting & mood: low-key, cool directional key light from upper left; soft rim light outlining the UI edges; gentle volumetric falloff; subtle reflections on glass and anodized metal. Calm, confident, structured vibe.

Color palette: deep navy/charcoal background, cool whites, restrained cyan accents, hints of muted violet for chart highlights; controlled saturation; no warm daylight tones.

Style: high-end product render / photoreal hybrid, ultra-clean, crisp UI lines, soft shadows, minimal clutter.

Camera: 50mm lens look, f/1.8 depth of field, slight bloom on highlights, high dynamic range, noise-free.

Include: small envelope icon motif embedded subtly in the UI; a thin progress bar and segmented metrics cards.

Avoid: bright white backgrounds, sunny lifestyle scenes, wood-heavy warmth, messy desks, visible real email content, legible logos, cartoon styling.

Output: 16:9 aspect ratio, 4k detail, premium dark UI aesthetic.

Optional placeholders: primary accent color = {ACCENT_COLOR (default: muted cyan)}; secondary accent = {SECONDARY_ACCENT (default: muted violet)}; device style = {DEVICE_STYLE (default: matte black)}

title: Turn Raw Feature Notes Into a Clear Product Value Proposition (with options)
content:
I’m going to paste messy notes about a product feature. Convert them into a crisp value proposition I can reuse across marketing and sales.

Input notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

Audience & context (if known):
- Target customer:
- Industry/use case:
- Pricing tier (free/pro/enterprise):
- Primary competitor / alternative:

Output (keep it modern, direct, and specific):
1) One-sentence value proposition (max 18 words)
2) 3 positioning angles (each with: headline + 2 bullet benefits)
3) “So what?” translation: map features → customer outcomes (table)
4) Objections + counters: 5 common objections with short, confident replies
5) Proof points I should collect (not claims): 6 items (metrics, testimonials, case study data, benchmarks)
6) Website hero set: headline, subhead, primary CTA, secondary CTA (2 variants)
7) Sales blurb: 75–100 words, no fluff
8) Tagline options: 12 punchy options, not cheesy

Rules:
- Don’t invent capabilities; if something is missing, write: “Need to confirm: ____”.
- Avoid buzzwords unless they add clarity (no “synergy,” “revolutionary,” “best-in-class”).
- Prefer concrete outcomes (time saved, errors reduced, revenue protected) over vague benefits.
- If my notes are contradictory, point it out and offer the cleanest assumption set.
- Keep sentences short. Make every line skimmable.

title: Turn a rough feature idea into a crisp UI spec (screen + states)
content:
I’m designing a UI for: [product/app]
Feature: [feature name]
Primary user: [who]
Core goal: [what they’re trying to do]
Platform: [web/ios/android]
Constraints: [brand, accessibility, tech, timeline]

Take my rough notes below and turn them into a clear, build-ready UI spec for ONE screen.

Rough notes:
[paste notes]

Output format (keep it concise, no fluff):
1) Screen purpose (1–2 sentences)
2) Information architecture: list the sections in top-to-bottom order
3) Component inventory: table with Component | Purpose | Default content | Variants/states (hover/active/disabled/loading/empty/error) | Notes
4) Interaction rules: bullet list of key behaviors (validation, confirmations, keyboard, gestures)
5) Edge cases: 6–10 bullets that a designer/engineer would actually miss
6) Acceptance criteria: 8–12 testable statements (Given/When/Then)

Guidelines:
- Prefer standard UI patterns over novelty.
- Call out any missing info as “Open questions” at the end (max 6), but still make reasonable assumptions.
- Write like you’re handing this to a product engineer tomorrow.

title: Campaign Ops + Attribution Router (UTMs, channels, owners, SLAs, dashboards)
content:
Act as my campaign-operations architect. Build a reusable routing + attribution system for marketing growth.

Context
- Company/product: {{company}}
- Primary goal: {{goal}} (e.g., pipeline, trials, purchases, retention)
- Geo/timezone: {{geo_tz}}
- Sales motion: {{sales_motion}} (PLG / sales-led / hybrid)
- CRM + MAP: {{crm_map}} (e.g., HubSpot + Salesforce)
- Source channels we use: {{channels}} (paid search, paid social, affiliates, email, SEO, partners, events, communities, etc.)
- Destinations: {{destinations}} (landing pages, app, checkout, demo form, webinar reg)
- Team/owners: {{teams_and_owners}} (names/roles or functions)
- Constraints: {{constraints}} (budget caps, compliance, sales territories, data retention)

Deliverable
Create an operational spec that I can hand to both marketing and analytics. Output in structured sections with tables where helpful.

1) Canonical UTM + Tracking Taxonomy
- Define required parameters (utm_source, utm_medium, utm_campaign, utm_content, utm_term) plus any additional keys we should standardize (e.g., utm_id, creative_id, adset_id, placement, matchtype).
- Provide allowed values and naming conventions (case, separators, date rules, campaign hierarchies).
- Include 8–12 examples across our channels showing “good” UTMs.
- Include rules for special cases: dark social, offline/event scans, QR codes, partner links, influencer codes, app-to-web, web-to-app.

2) Source-of-Truth Mapping (Attribution Inputs)
- Define how we map raw inbound data into:
  - Channel Group
  - Campaign
  - Initiative/Theme
  - Offer
  - Audience/ICP segment
  - Geo/territory
- Provide a mapping table with:
  - Incoming signal (UTM/referrer/platform field)
  - Transformation rule (regex/lookup)
  - Output field
  - Fallback when missing
- Specify precedence order when fields conflict (e.g., gclid vs UTMs, platform click IDs vs referrer).

3) Lead/Signup Routing Logic (Decision Tree)
- Build a decision tree that assigns each inbound lead/signup to:
  - Owner/team
  - Priority (P0/P1/P2)
  - SLA (time-to-first-touch)
  - Next action (sequence, call, nurture, product prompt)
- Inputs to consider: channel, intent, form type, company size, job title, geo, existing account match, product usage, partner source.
- Include dedupe/merge rules (email/domain matching), and what happens when data is incomplete.

4) Lifecycle + Conversion Definitions
- Define lifecycle stages and the exact entry/exit criteria (MQL/SQL/Opportunity/Activated/Retained—tailor to our goal).
- Provide event instrumentation checklist for each destination (landing page, form, app events, checkout).
- Specify how we handle multi-touch vs last-touch, and recommend a default attribution model with justification.

5) Operational Workflow (Campaign Intake → Launch → QA → Reporting)
- Create a lightweight process with roles, required fields, and approvals.
- Include:
  - Campaign intake form fields
  - Pre-launch QA checklist (links, UTMs, pixels, events, consent, CRM field capture)
  - Post-launch monitoring checks (spend anomalies, conversion drops)
  - Weekly hygiene tasks (taxonomy audits, broken UTMs, unmapped sources)

6) Dashboard + Reporting Schema
- Define a minimal data model (tables/fields) for reporting: Sessions/Clicks, Leads/Signups, Opportunities/Revenue, Costs.
- Provide the KPI set by funnel stage, with formulas.
- Specify breakdowns that must always work (channel group, campaign, geo, device, landing page, audience).
- Include a reconciliation plan between ad platforms, analytics, and CRM.

7) Output Artifacts
Return the following artifacts in your final answer:
- A “UTM Standard” one-pager (bullets)
- A mapping rules table (operator-ready)
- A routing decision tree (readable text or pseudo-logic)
- A launch QA checklist
- A dashboard field dictionary

Constraints
- Keep it practical; avoid vendor-specific steps unless I name tools.
- Prefer deterministic rules over vague guidance.
- Call out assumptions explicitly.

If anything essential is missing, ask up to 5 clarifying questions; otherwise proceed with reasonable defaults and label them as defaults.
